story.text: Hi! This is a map of more than 100,000 happy moments.

These moments were gathered during a [2017 research project](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322695424_HappyDB_A_Corpus_of_100000_Crowdsourced_Happy_Moments). I wanted to understand what makes people happy. So I mapped these moments on two axes.
In the **north ↑**, are experiences that fulfill **immediate** desires—things like eating your favorite food or going to the movies.

But researchers have [found](https://www.tandfonline.com/doi/abs/10.1080/17439760.2013.830764) that happiness is different from living a meaningful life: “The quest for meaning is a key part of what makes us human, and uniquely so.” This requires us to have a cohesive story about our past, present, and future.
So in the **south ↓** are experiences that take a **longer time** to come to fruition—things like receiving a college degree.

One of the most important factors for living a happy and meaningful life is our relationships. Harvard researchers have been [following](https://www.adultdevelopmentstudy.org/grantandglueckstudy) a group of people, and their kids, since the 1930s. The [findings](https://www.ted.com/talks/robert_waldinger_what_makes_a_good_life_lessons_from_the_longest_study_on_happiness) are quite clear: Good relationships are critical for living happy, healthy lives.
So on the **← west ** side, are moments that we can’t trigger on our own—like receiving affection or a gift.

But intentionally being alone is also quite important. It helps [regulate](https://www.durham.ac.uk/research/current/thought-leadership/2023/04/the-importance-of-solitude--why-time-on-your-own-can-sometimes-be-good-for-you/) our emotions, while helping us [develop](https://experts.illinois.edu/en/publications/the-solitary-side-of-life-an-examination-of-the-time-people-spend/) our identity.
So in the **east →** are experiences that we have more control over, often done alone—things like partaking in a hobby or buying a new car.
The world feels particularly broken right now—filled with suffering, chaos, and violence. It feels a bit off to focus on a story about happiness.

But our fight for survival has to be oriented toward protecting the universal human desire to pursue happiness and meaning. That’s what the fascists want to take from us. That’s what we’re fighting to protect.
Explore these moments. Filter the responses using the button on the top-right.

info:
In 2017, more than 10,000 people tracked experiences that made them happy as part of a research project. The researchers gathered more than 100,000 happy moments.

Data methodology

I initially used the gemini-2.0-flash-001 LLM model to determine how much an agency score and time score (immediate vs. long-term), as well as a brief summary of the happy moment. These results helped me create a three-level topic taxonomy that is analogous to continents, countries, and states; there are obviously many ways to group these moments, so this is reflective of how I think about happy moments. I then prompted gemini-2.0-flash-001 to categorize each happy moment into the three-level taxonomy. I cleaned up the results in [Open Refine](https://openrefine.org/) to ensure there is no cross-taxonomy categorization; for example, the LLM sometimes put a moment into a state that is not within the proper parent country or continent. (This error is akin to saying that a moment belongs in New York state, and in the country of France.) From there, I used gemini-2.0-flash-001 to give each moment a score of how well it fits into its assigned categories, since sometimes moments didn't have a perfect category in the taxonomy; what you are more likely to see on the screen on the best fits.

Map methodology

The map is initially split up into 200,000 regions using a [voronoi](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Voronoi_diagram) algorithm; it looks like a giraffe's fur. From there, the top-level category—the “continents”—are initially given a starting atom, based on the average agency and time scores. It is then allowed to take over atoms based on the countries, states, and cities (moments) that it needs to fit within its bounds. That conquering process determined what the map looks like. This ultimately means that the moments mostly follow the x- and y-axis of time and agency, like a scatterplot. But since two things can't occupy the same space, it's not a perfect scatterplot. The moments are then placed on the map with illustrations of people. The people were drawn on paper with an ink pen and fountain pen, then digitized so they could be used as icons.

Citation

Akari Asai, Sara Evensen, Behzad Golshan, Alon Halevy, Vivian Li, Andrei Lopatenko, Daniela Stepanov, Yoshihiko Suhara, Wang-Chiew Tan, Yinzhan Xu, "HappyDB: A Corpus of 100,000 Crowdsourced Happy Moments", LREC '18, May 2018. | [Paper](https://www.researchgate.net/publication/322695424_HappyDB_A_Corpus_of_100000_Crowdsourced_Happy_Moments) | [Data](https://megagonlabs.github.io/HappyDB/)
text:
Every once in a while, someone asks me a question that catches me offguard: “Are you happy?”


I don’t think of myself as a happy person. I struggle to enjoy ephemeral moments of joy. But when I think about what matters to me—and whether I’m on a journey that aligns with those values—the answer is yes. So I split the difference, and I asked: “I’m OK.”
